skill.name: openclaw-medical-skills
skill.description: Routes biomedical, clinical, genomics, drug discovery, bioinformatics, public health, medical device, and regulatory tasks through the OpenClaw Medical Skills capability index.
skill.summary: Route medical and biomedical research tasks to OpenClaw capabilities with safety, privacy, and evidence standards.
skill.author: Yuan1z0825

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high and critical alerts, but review found the credential and weak-cryptography matches are biomedical terminology, markdown tables, and historical install examples. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found. The skill still warrants a medium risk rating because it supports high-stakes medical decision support, external source use, shell tooling, and local health or genomic data handling.

medium_findings.title: High-Stakes Medical Decision Support Scope
medium_findings.description: The skill routes clinical, drug, genomics, trial, imaging, and regulatory work. The main instructions include safety boundaries, but outputs could influence patient care if users ignore those boundaries.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly cover clinical decision support, drug interaction, patient matching, and guideline workflows. The presence of safety language lowers malicious-risk confidence but confirms high-stakes use.

medium_findings.title: Local Health and Genomic Data Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill can use local files, Python, R, shell tools, spreadsheets, and biomedical pipelines. This is legitimate for research, but it may process protected health information or sensitive genomic data if users provide it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions directly authorize local files and computational tools, and the privacy section acknowledges clinical documents and protected health information. The risk depends on user data and runtime permissions.

low_findings.title: Static Credential Alerts Are Biomedical SAM/BAM References
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged Windows SAM database patterns, but the reviewed lines refer to SAM/BAM genomic alignment files and samtools workflows. No evidence found of Windows credential database access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines use genomics terms such as SAM/BAM/CRAM, pysam, and samtools. This context clearly distinguishes them from Windows account credential files.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Cryptography Alerts Are Terminology Matches
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but sampled lines contain clinical trial phases, DESeq2, ComBat, Primer3, CRISPR design, LaTeX, and other biomedical or writing terms. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed examples are markdown descriptions of biomedical capabilities, not code that hashes, encrypts, or signs data. The broad repeated pattern is consistent with keyword collision.

low_findings.title: Historical Install Commands and URLs Are Not Runtime Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The source reference preserves OpenClaw installation examples, GitHub badge URLs, acknowledgements, and clone or copy commands. They are documentation and provenance, not executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are inside markdown code fences or link lists in the preserved upstream README. The active SKILL.md says to treat OpenClaw and NanoClaw install instructions as historical context.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Commands and Local Analysis Tools May Be Used
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill tells Codex to translate capabilities into local files, Python, R, shell tools, spreadsheets, and browser or web verification. This is expected for bioinformatics, but users should review commands before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicitly present in the skill workflow and source examples. It is legitimate but could be unsafe with untrusted inputs or overly broad filesystem permissions.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Medical and Scientific Sources May Be Queried
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends official or primary sources and references many biomedical databases and APIs. Network use is expected, but patient data should not be sent externally without explicit user approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly mention external databases, APIs, and source verification. I found no exfiltration endpoint, so the risk is privacy-sensitive network use rather than malicious traffic.

content.value_statement: Biomedical and clinical tasks often require the right database, workflow, and safety boundary. This skill routes requests through a large medical capability index and adds evidence, privacy, and reproducibility standards.
actual_capabilities: Routes clinical, biomedical, genomics, drug, imaging, public health, and regulatory requests to named OpenClaw capabilities.
Uses the capability index to identify relevant tools, source types, and workflow boundaries before execution.
Guides evidence synthesis with primary medical, regulatory, label, trial, and scientific sources.
Supports reproducible bioinformatics and data analysis planning with tool choices, parameters, QC checks, and output provenance.
Adds safety guidance for patient-facing summaries, urgent symptoms, medication changes, genetics, and imaging interpretation.
Preserves upstream OpenClaw capability descriptions for provenance and broad task discovery.
limitations: It is not a licensed clinician, pharmacist, genetic counselor, or regulatory authority.
Some OpenClaw-specific APIs, MCP servers, or databases may be unavailable in a Codex session.
Current medical facts require web verification from official or primary sources.
User-provided health, genomic, or clinical documents must be de-identified when possible.

use_cases.title: Draft a clinical research evidence brief
use_cases.description: Map a disease, intervention, or biomarker question to literature, guideline, trial, and safety evidence with clear limitations.

use_cases.title: Plan a reproducible genomics workflow
use_cases.description: Select relevant bioinformatics capabilities for VCF, FASTQ, BAM, single-cell, or multi-omics analysis and define QC outputs.

use_cases.title: Prepare regulated medical documentation
use_cases.description: Draft checklists, evidence tables, and document outlines for medical device, clinical trial, or quality system work.

prompt_templates.title: Find the right capability
prompt_templates.prompt: Use OpenClaw Medical Skills to identify the best capability for this biomedical task: [describe task]. Explain why it fits and list any missing inputs.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize clinical evidence safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Use OpenClaw Medical Skills to create an evidence brief on [condition or intervention]. Include source types, key findings, uncertainty, and safety caveats.

prompt_templates.title: Design a bioinformatics analysis plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use OpenClaw Medical Skills to plan an analysis for [file types and research question]. Include inputs, tools, QC checks, parameters, and expected outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Build an auditable regulatory package outline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use OpenClaw Medical Skills to outline a regulatory or quality package for [product and jurisdiction]. Include standards, evidence needs, assumptions, and review checkpoints.

output_examples.input: Find capabilities for matching oncology patients to trials using variants and location.
output_examples.output: The skill would select trial matching, precision oncology, and variant interpretation capabilities. It would request diagnosis, biomarkers, stage, prior therapy, location, and source dates before ranking trials.

output_examples.input: Plan analysis for paired tumor-normal VCF files.
output_examples.output: The skill would propose variant annotation, cancer variant interpretation, quality checks, genome build confirmation, database versions, and a reproducible report with assumptions and review needs.

output_examples.input: Draft a medical device software risk checklist.
output_examples.output: The skill would identify jurisdiction, intended use, risk class, lifecycle phase, and applicable standards. It would produce a draft checklist for regulatory and quality review.

best_practices: Provide de-identified inputs, source provenance, dates, units, and file formats before analysis.
Ask for official or primary-source verification when facts may affect care, regulation, labels, or trials.
Treat outputs as drafts or decision support and route patient-specific conclusions to qualified professionals.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill as a definitive diagnosis, prescription, treatment order, or emergency response system.
Do not send identifiable patient or genomic data to external services without explicit approval and privacy review.
Do not run generated shell, Python, R, or bioinformatics commands on untrusted files without reviewing inputs and outputs.

faq.question: Can this skill provide medical advice?
faq.answer: It can support education, research, drafting, and decision support. It cannot replace a qualified clinician or provide definitive patient care.

faq.question: Does it connect to live medical databases?
faq.answer: It can recommend and use primary sources or available connectors when the session permits. Some upstream OpenClaw APIs may not be available.

faq.question: Can it analyze genomic or bioinformatics files?
faq.answer: Yes, it can plan and guide analyses for formats such as VCF, FASTQ, BAM, CRAM, h5ad, and related omics files.

faq.question: How should sensitive health data be handled?
faq.answer: Minimize protected health information and de-identify names, exact dates, addresses, and personal identifiers when possible.

faq.question: Is it useful for regulatory work?
faq.answer: Yes, it can draft checklists and evidence outlines for medical devices, trials, quality systems, and regulated documents.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The review found no malicious code, but the skill handles high-stakes medical workflows and may use local tools, files, and external sources.
